name: Dependency Inversion Principle
source: Clean Code by Robert C. Martin, Chapter 10: Classes
problem: High-level classes directly depend on low-level concrete classes, so changing a low-level implementation forces changes in the high-level business logic and makes the system impossible to test in isolation.
steps: Identify high-level classes that directly instantiate or import low-level classes.
Extract an interface or abstract class representing the abstraction the high-level class needs.
Make the high-level class depend on the abstraction, not the concrete implementation.
Inject the concrete implementation through the constructor or a setter (Dependency Injection).
Verify that high-level business logic can be tested with a mock or stub injected in place of the real dependency.
